title: OFAC Designates Networks Supplying Weapons and UAV Components to Iran
summary: The U.S. Treasury's OFAC designated multiple entities and individuals on May 8 under non-proliferation and Iran-related authorities for supplying weapons and UAV components to Iran, targeting Chinese and Belarusian networks linked to Iran's Center for Innovation and Technology Cooperation.
contents:
## OFAC Non-Proliferation Designations Target Iran Weapons Supply Networks

### Action Overview
The U.S. Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) announced designations on May 8, 2026, targeting networks that supply weapons and unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) components to Iran. The action, titled "Economic Fury Disrupts Networks Supplying Weapons and UAV Components to Iran," falls under both non-proliferation and Iran-related sanctions authorities.

### Designated Entities and Individuals
OFAC added three individuals to the Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) List:
- **Li Genping** (China) — linked to Hitex Insulation Ningbo Company Limited
- **Mohammadmahdi Maleki** (Iran, based in Belarus) — connected to Iran's Center for Innovation and Technology Cooperation (CITC)
- **Mohammed Ali Tolibov** (Belarus) — associated with Armory Alliance LLC

Multiple entities received designations, including:
- **AE International Trade Co Limited** (Hong Kong)
- **Armory Alliance LLC** (Belarus)
- **Hitex Insulation Ningbo Company Limited** (China)
- **Meentropy Technology Hangzhou Co Ltd** (China) — identified under Iran conventional arms authority
- **The Earth Eye Co** (Beijing) — designated under Iran conventional arms authority

### Strategic Significance
These designations represent a continued expansion of the "Economic Fury" campaign, now extending beyond oil trade sanctions to target the physical supply chain of weapons technology flowing to Iran. The inclusion of Chinese and Belarusian intermediaries highlights the multi-jurisdictional nature of Iran's procurement networks for military components, particularly drone and satellite technology firms.

### Impact Assessment
The designations freeze all U.S.-based assets of listed parties and prohibit U.S. persons from transacting with them. Secondary sanctions risks apply to foreign entities continuing business relationships with designated parties. The action directly targets Iran's ability to reconstitute its drone fleet and acquire surveillance technology during the ongoing conflict.

title: Three IDF Reservists Wounded by Hezbollah Explosive Drone in Northern Israel
summary: An explosive drone launched by Hezbollah struck northern Israel near the Lebanese border on May 9, seriously wounding one IDF reservist and moderately injuring two others, amid ongoing ceasefire violations in southern Lebanon.
contents:
## Hezbollah Drone Strike Wounds Three IDF Reservists

### Incident Details
On May 9, 2026, Hezbollah launched an explosive drone that struck Israeli territory close to the border with Lebanon. The attack wounded three IDF reservists — one sustained serious injuries while a reservist officer and another reservist soldier suffered moderate wounds. All three troops received evacuation to a hospital, and their families received notification of the incident.

### Additional Engagements
In a separate incident on the same day, an explosive drone struck an unmanned engineering vehicle operating in southern Lebanon, causing material damage but no casualties. The IDF also reported that air defense systems intercepted several rockets that Hezbollah fired at troops positioned in southern Lebanon.

### Ceasefire Context
The attacks occurred despite a nominal ceasefire agreement between Israel and Hezbollah. The Israeli military earlier on May 9 issued forced displacement orders to residents of nine villages near Tyre, Sidon, and Nabatiyeh in southern Lebanon, citing continued Hezbollah violations. The IDF announced it struck 85 Hezbollah targets over the preceding 24 hours, including positions near Nabatiyeh, al-Mansouri, Burj Rahhal, and Nabatiyeh al-Fawqa.

### Broader Pattern
This drone strike demonstrates Hezbollah's continued capability to penetrate Israeli airspace despite expanded air defense deployments. The group has increasingly relied on drone warfare to challenge Israeli operations in southern Lebanon, testing multi-layered defense systems across the border region.

title: North Korea Announces Deployment of New Long-Range Artillery Targeting Seoul
summary: North Korea announced on May 9 plans to deploy new 155mm self-propelled gun-howitzers with 60km striking range along the southern border this year, alongside commissioning its first naval destroyer in coming weeks, marking a significant escalation in peninsular military posture.
contents:
## North Korea Escalates Military Deployments Along Southern Border

### New Artillery Systems
North Korean leader Kim Jong Un visited a munitions factory on May 7 to inspect the production of 155mm self-propelled gun-howitzers scheduled for deployment at a southern border artillery unit within 2026. The Korean Central News Agency (KCNA) reported Kim stating the striking range of this large-caliber rifled gun exceeds 60 kilometers (37 miles), placing the entirety of Seoul's metropolitan area — home to approximately 10 million people — within direct fire range.

Kim declared that "such a rapid extension of striking range and remarkable improvement of striking capability will provide a great change and advantage in the land operations of our army." Various operational and tactical missile systems and powerful multiple rocket launcher systems also face scheduled deployment along the border.

### Naval Commissioning
On May 7, Kim rode aboard the destroyer Choe Hyon to review its maneuverability off North Korea's west coast. He ordered the vessel handed over to the navy by mid-June. KCNA photographs showed Kim's teenage daughter — whom South Korea's spy service identified last month as a potential heir — accompanying him aboard the ship.

### Constitutional Changes
The announcement followed South Korea's revelation that North Korea's newly revised constitution drops all references to Korean unification, codifying Kim's vow to establish a permanent two-state system on the Korean Peninsula and designating South Korea as North Korea's "most hostile enemy."

### Regional Security Implications
The new artillery deployments complement North Korea's existing forward-deployed artillery force near the DMZ, which already poses a significant conventional threat to Seoul. The combination of extended-range guns, missile systems, and rocket launchers along the border represents a qualitative improvement in Pyongyang's ability to hold Seoul at risk without resorting to ballistic missiles subject to UN Security Council prohibitions.

title: US Disables Two Iranian Oil Tankers Attempting to Breach Naval Blockade
summary: US Central Command announced that a Navy fighter jet disabled two Iranian-flagged tankers in the Gulf of Oman on May 8 by firing precision munitions into their smokestacks, as the fragile month-old ceasefire with Iran faces growing strain from repeated military exchanges.
contents:
## US Forces Disable Iranian Tankers Amid Fragile Ceasefire

### Engagement Details
US Central Command reported on May 8 that a US Navy fighter jet disabled two unladen Iranian-flagged oil tankers, the Sea Star III and Sevda, in the Gulf of Oman after both vessels attempted to enter an Iranian port in violation of the US naval blockade. The jet fired precision munitions into the smokestacks of both vessels, and CENTCOM released unclassified video of the strikes.

### Escalating Exchanges
The tanker strikes followed a broader exchange of fire on May 8 in the Strait of Hormuz, where US forces reported intercepting Iranian attacks on three Navy ships involving speedboats, missiles, and drones. The US military responded with strikes on Iranian military facilities along the strait. Iran's Foreign Ministry condemned the actions as a "clear violation" of the ceasefire.

The UAE Defense Ministry reported on May 9 that Emirati air defenses engaged two Iranian ballistic missiles and three drones, with three people wounded. A US strike also killed at least one sailor and injured 10 others aboard a cargo vessel that caught fire.

### Blockade Operations
CENTCOM stated that US naval forces have redirected 57 vessels since the blockade began on April 13 and currently prevent more than 70 tankers from entering or departing Iranian ports. These vessels collectively hold more than 166 million barrels of Iranian oil, valued at approximately $13 billion.

### Diplomatic Status
Despite the military exchanges, President Trump maintained the ceasefire remains in effect and reiterated threats to resume full-scale bombing if Iran rejects a deal. Secretary of State Rubio stated he expected an Iranian response to the latest US proposal. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson said Tehran continues reviewing the proposal without regard to "deadlines."

title: Bahrain Arrests 41 Suspects Linked to Iran's Revolutionary Guard
summary: Bahrain's Ministry of Interior announced the arrest of 41 individuals on May 9 for alleged affiliation with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, amid ongoing regional tensions and Bahrain's broader crackdown on pro-Iran sentiment during the US-Iran conflict.
contents:
## Bahrain Detains 41 in IRGC-Linked Security Operation

### Arrest Operation
Bahrain's Ministry of Interior announced on May 9, 2026, the arrest of 41 individuals alleged to form part of a group affiliated with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC). The ministry stated that investigations continue to identify and pursue additional individuals connected to the network. Authorities provided no further operational details regarding the timing, locations, or specific allegations against the detained individuals.

### Context of Crackdown
The arrests represent part of Bahrain's intensified security posture since the outbreak of the US-Iran war on February 28, 2026. The island kingdom, which hosts the US Navy's Fifth Fleet headquarters, has conducted systematic arrests of residents and foreigners who filmed Iranian strikes during the conflict. Authorities charged dozens with misusing social media, inciting hatred, or treason — an offense carrying a potential death sentence.

Bahrain's government also stripped citizenship from 69 individuals for allegedly expressing "sympathy and praise for Iran's hostile and criminal acts," extending the measure to family members of the accused.

### Strategic Significance
The Sunni-ruled monarchy governs a majority-Shiite population and experienced pro-Iran demonstrations early in the conflict. Human rights organizations have criticized the kingdom for leveraging the war as justification for suppressing domestic dissent. The arrests highlight the domestic security dimensions of the broader regional conflict, where Gulf states hosting US military facilities face internal pressures from populations with sectarian ties to Iran.

### Regional Alignment
Bahrain's actions align with the UAE's increasingly confrontational stance toward Iran. Both nations have strengthened security cooperation with the United States and Israel while facing direct Iranian military threats, including ballistic missile and drone attacks throughout the conflict.

title: CENTCOM Reports US Naval Forces Have Redirected 57 Vessels in Iran Blockade Since April 13
summary: US Central Command reported on May 8 that naval forces have redirected 57 vessels since the Iran port blockade began on April 13, while currently preventing more than 70 tankers holding over 166 million barrels of Iranian oil from transiting.
contents:
## US Naval Blockade of Iran Reaches Significant Scale

### Operational Summary
US Central Command (CENTCOM) announced on May 8, 2026, that US naval forces have redirected 57 vessels since the blockade of Iran's ports began on April 13. The command reported that more than 70 tankers remain prevented from entering or departing Iranian ports, collectively holding over 166 million barrels of Iranian oil valued at approximately $13 billion.

### Latest Enforcement Actions
On May 8, CENTCOM reported that US forces disabled two Iranian tankers — Sea Star III and Sevda — after both attempted to enter an Iranian port along the Gulf of Oman. A US Navy fighter jet fired precision munitions into their smokestacks. Earlier in the week, an American military jet shot out the rudder of another tanker attempting to breach the blockade.

These enforcement actions occurred alongside exchanges of fire in the Strait of Hormuz, where CENTCOM stated it intercepted Iranian attacks involving speedboats, missiles, and drones targeting three US Navy ships. The command responded with strikes against "Iranian military facilities responsible for attacking US forces."

### Strategic Context
The naval blockade represents a central pillar of the US strategy to pressure Iran into accepting terms to end the conflict and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has maintained its own effective closure of the strait since the war began on February 28, cutting off approximately 20% of global crude oil transit and triggering sustained global energy price increases.

### Force Posture
US naval forces continue to maintain a significant presence across the Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman. The blockade operations require sustained carrier strike group deployments, surface combatant patrols, and maritime surveillance to enforce the restriction on vessel movements to and from Iranian ports.

title: PLA Sends Eight Military Aircraft Near Taiwan With All Crossing Median Line
summary: Taiwan's Ministry of National Defence detected 8 PLA aircraft sorties, 6 PLAN vessels, and 2 official ships operating around Taiwan on May 9, with all 8 aircraft crossing the Taiwan Strait median line and entering Taiwan's central, southwestern, and eastern ADIZ sectors.
contents:
## PLA Military Activity Intensifies Around Taiwan

### May 9 Detection Summary
Taiwan's Ministry of National Defence reported on May 9, 2026, that it detected 8 sorties of People's Liberation Army (PLA) aircraft, 6 PLA Navy (PLAN) vessels, and 2 official ships operating around Taiwan up until 6:00 a.m. local time (UTC+8). All 8 aircraft sorties crossed the Taiwan Strait median line and entered Taiwan's central, southwestern, and eastern Air Defense Identification Zone (ADIZ) sectors. The ROC Armed Forces monitored the situation and responded accordingly.

### Continued Pattern
The activity followed an even more intense period on May 8, when Taiwan detected 12 PLA aircraft sorties, 6 PLAN vessels, and 2 official ships, with 10 of 12 sorties crossing the median line and entering northern, southwestern, and eastern ADIZ sectors. The sustained pattern of median-line crossings represents a normalization of provocative military posture around the island.

### Defense Budget Breakthrough
The military activity coincided with Taiwan's parliament approving a $25 billion special defense spending bill on May 8 after months of political gridlock. The budget, which falls short of the government's proposed $40 billion, funds US weapons acquisitions including HIMARS rocket systems but excludes domestically developed drones and the Chiang Kung anti-ballistic missile program. The US State Department responded by calling further delays to remaining capabilities a "concession to the Chinese Communist Party."

### Strategic Assessment
The average monthly number of Chinese military aircraft sorties around Taiwan fell by more than half in Q1 2026 compared to early periods of President Lai's administration. However, sustained daily crossings of the median line — once considered a de facto buffer zone — demonstrate the PLA's continued campaign to erode established norms in the Taiwan Strait. The activity occurs ahead of a scheduled Trump-Xi summit in Beijing on May 14-15.

links.label: Asianet - PLA Aircraft Near Taiwan May 9

title: Libya's Largest Oil Refinery Shuts Down as Armed Clashes Engulf Zawiya
summary: Libya's National Oil Corporation halted operations at the Zawiya oil refinery, the country's largest operational facility with 120,000 bpd capacity, after armed clashes between rival groups sent heavy weapons projectiles into the oil complex on May 8-9.
contents:
## Zawiya Refinery Shutdown Amid Armed Clashes in Western Libya

### Emergency Shutdown
Libya's National Oil Corporation (NOC) and the Zawiya Oil Refining Company announced a precautionary halt to operations at the Zawiya oil refinery after armed clashes erupted in the facility's vicinity during the early hours of May 9, 2026. The refinery, located approximately 40 kilometers west of Tripoli, holds a processing capacity of 120,000 barrels per day and connects to Libya's 300,000 bpd Sharara oilfield.

### Damage Assessment
The NOC confirmed that "several heavy weapons projectiles" landed in various locations within the oil complex. While no significant structural damage materialized initially, the fighting intensified and spread to residential areas adjacent to the refinery, placing the entire complex under direct threat of heavy shelling. All tankers received evacuation orders from the port, and the refinery suspended operations completely.

### Security Operation
The Zawiya Security Directorate and Joint Security Room announced a "large-scale security operation" targeting what authorities described as "criminal hideouts and wanted individuals." The operation, launched on prosecutor's orders, targeted suspects involved in forming criminal gangs, murder, kidnapping, extortion, drug trafficking, arms trafficking, people smuggling, and illegal migration. Fire-fighting crews remained at the facility while all other personnel, including students at the nearby Petroleum Institute, received evacuation orders.

### Strategic Impact
Libya's oil production reached approximately 1.43 million barrels per day during April-May 2026 — the highest output in over a decade. The Zawiya shutdown threatens this recovery at a critical moment when global demand for Libyan crude has surged due to the Strait of Hormuz disruption. The NOC stated that fuel supplies to Tripoli and surrounding areas remained unaffected despite the refinery closure.

title: Putin Oversees Scaled-Back Victory Day Parade as Three-Day Ukraine Ceasefire Holds
summary: Russia held a scaled-back Victory Day parade on May 9 with no tanks or military vehicles, featuring North Korean troops marching for the first time, while a US-brokered three-day ceasefire with Ukraine and a 1,000-prisoner exchange appeared to hold.
contents:
## Russia's Victory Day Parade Reflects Wartime Pressures

### Scaled-Back Commemoration
Russian President Vladimir Putin oversaw the 81st anniversary Victory Day military parade on Red Square on May 9, 2026, marking the Soviet Union's defeat of Nazi Germany. The event proceeded in a significantly reduced format — no tanks or other military equipment rolled across Red Square's cobblestones, marking a stark departure from the massive military showcases of previous years.

Soldiers, sailors, and veterans marched as Putin sat beneath Vladimir Lenin's Mausoleum. Kremlin aide Yuri Ushakov described the event as "everything as usual, except for the demonstration of military equipment."

### North Korean Troops March
For the first time, North Korean troops participated in the Victory Day parade, a tribute to Pyongyang's deployment of soldiers to fight alongside Russian forces repelling a Ukrainian incursion into Russia's Kursk region. Their presence underscored the deepening Russia-North Korea military relationship.

### Three-Day Ceasefire
The parade coincided with a US-brokered three-day ceasefire running from Saturday through Monday, supported by both the Kremlin and Kyiv. The two sides also agreed to exchange 1,000 prisoners of war. President Trump expressed hope the ceasefire could represent "the beginning of the end" of the conflict and urged "a big extension."

No reports of ceasefire violations emerged from either Moscow or Kyiv during the parade. Russia had warned that any Ukrainian attempt to disrupt the celebrations would trigger a massive missile strike on the Ukrainian capital. Ukrainian President Zelenskyy issued a tongue-in-cheek decree "allowing" the parade to proceed.

### Security and Internet Restrictions
Moscow deployed extensive security measures including soldiers with guns atop pickup trucks, blocked roads around the capital center, and restricted mobile internet and SMS messaging to counter potential Ukrainian drone attacks. The internet restrictions covered the Russian government's "white list" of approved online services.

title: Taiwan Parliament Approves $25 Billion Special Defense Spending Bill After Months of Gridlock
summary: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan approved a $25 billion special defense budget on May 8, well short of the government's proposed $40 billion, with the US State Department warning that further delays to remaining capabilities constitute a 'concession to the Chinese Communist Party.'
contents:
## Taiwan Breaks Defense Budget Deadlock

### Legislative Approval
Taiwan's Legislative Yuan approved a $25 billion special defense spending bill on May 8, 2026, ending months of partisan gridlock between the ruling Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) and opposition Kuomintang (KMT) and Taiwan People's Party (TPP). Parliamentary Speaker Han Kuo-yu announced the final vote result, clearing funding that falls well short of the government's proposed $40 billion budget.

### Scope and Exclusions
The approved budget funds acquisitions of US weapons systems, including the Lockheed Martin-manufactured High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS), which had experienced payment delays during the legislative standoff. However, the bill excludes several critical domestic programs:
- The **Chiang Kung (Strong Bow) anti-ballistic missile**, intended as the backbone of Taiwan's new T-Dome air defense system
- **Sea attack drone systems** and other asymmetric warfare capabilities
- **Domestic defense manufacturing** investments

Taiwan's Defense Ministry warned that the exclusions will "severely impact" air defense combat effectiveness and create "capability gaps" that affect "the integrity of the overall defense system."

### US Response
The US State Department stated that while encouraged by the budget's passage, "further delays in funding the remaining proposed capabilities constitute a concession to the Chinese Communist Party." US officials have intensified lobbying efforts, with Indo-Pacific Commander Admiral Paparo and AIT Director Greene both urging prompt passage of the full defense package.

### Strategic Timing
The budget approval arrives ahead of a planned Trump-Xi summit in Beijing on May 14-15, where Taiwan arms sales rank among top agenda items. President Lai Ching-te acknowledged the approved funding as a necessary first step while warning that "any gap will affect the integrity of the overall defense system" and "any delay will increase the shared security risks borne by the people of Taiwan."

links.label: Japan Times - Taiwan Defense Budget Passes
